{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].

However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dropping {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).

The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.

The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes approaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dropping {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office)
== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes approaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dropping {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes approaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dropping {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office)
== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes approaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dropping {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (aqqroximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], aqqroximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes aqqroaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), droqqing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (aqqroximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), droqqing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (aqqroximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office)
== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], aqqroximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes aqqroaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), droqqing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (aqqroximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office)
== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.

At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes aqqroaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), droqqing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (aqqroximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], aqqroximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes aqqroaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), droqqing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (aqqroximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], [[Category:pp error]]approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office)
== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], [[Category:pp error]]approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]].
