At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office)== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], [[Category:pp error]]approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office)== See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|天守}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office) == See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], [[Category:pp error]]approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office) == See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

revision.text:
{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==

== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office) == See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|ja|日本本土空襲}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Gifu Air Raid|ja|岐阜空襲}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Sendai Air Raid|ja|仙台空襲}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|air raids on Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|ja|アメリカ陸軍航空軍}} during [[World War II]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|strategic bombing|ja|戦略爆撃}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Wakayama City|ja|和歌山市}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade Town|ja|岩出市}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa Town|ja|粉河町}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅}}<ref group="注釈">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[August 14]], [[Category:pp error]]approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|B-29 Superfortress|ja|B-29_(航空機)}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|West Field|ja|テニアン島|Tinian}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|ja|紀ノ川駅}}, {{ill|Wakayama City Station|ja|和歌山市駅}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo ([[Category:pp error]]approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

revision.text:
{{Pathnav|第二次世界大戦|太平洋戦争|日本本土の戦い|日本本土空襲|frame=1}} '''和歌山大空襲'''（わかやまだいくうしゅう）は、[[第二次世界大戦]]中[[アメリカ軍]]により行われた[[和歌山市|和歌山]]に対する一連の[[空襲]]の通称。規模が最も大きい[[1945年]][[7月9日]]深夜から[[7月10日]]未明にかけて行われた[[和歌山県]][[和歌山市]]中心部への[[空襲]]（[[戦略爆撃]]）を指すことが多い。
== 空襲の経緯 == === 1945年7月9日以前の空襲 === [[1942年]]4月、[[アメリカ軍]]による[[和歌山県]]に対する初めての[[空襲]]が、岩出町（現[[岩出市]]）や[[粉河町]]（現[[紀の川市]]）付近で行われた。
[[1945年]][[1月9日]]、[[和歌山駅|東和歌山駅]]<ref group="注釈">[[1968年]]に和歌山駅に改称</ref>付近を[[爆撃]]された。以降、[[8月14日]]までの間に10数回の爆撃が行われた。
また、和歌山県内各地でも、沿岸部の都市の港湾施設や石油関連施設及び内陸部の都市が同様な[[戦略爆撃]]や[[機銃掃射]]を受けていた。
=== 7月9日の空襲 === 1945年[[7月9日]]17時頃、[[テニアン島|テニアン]]西飛行場を多数の[[B-29_(航空機)|B-29]]と1機の気象観測機が離陸し出撃した。21時10分、[[和歌山市]]全域と紀州沖に警戒警報が出され、[[ラジオ]]はアメリカ軍機が熊野沖に飛来したことを報じていたものの、なかなか飛来しないため市民はやや油断していたが、22時25分に和歌山県全域に空襲警報が発令された。そして、22時30分には紀州沖海域にも空襲警報が発令された。23時頃、ラジオが「敵爆撃機、約250機、5群に分かれて[[紀伊水道]]を北上。[[淡路島]]上空で旋回し、1群は南東方面に向かった」と報じた。
アメリカ軍は[[高度]]10,200～11,600[[フィート]]（約3,100～3,500m）より和歌山市上空に進入し、23時36分に河西部の湊河口付近へ[[照明弾]]を落として、爆撃を開始した。その後、アメリカ軍は[[紀ノ川駅]]周辺を爆撃し、[[和歌山市駅]]、[[ぶらくり丁商店街|ぶらくり丁]]、[[和歌山県庁舎]]付近、[[和歌山市役所]]付近などに[[焼夷弾]]や[[油脂弾]]を落としたため、中心部はほぼ壊滅状態になった（7割が壊滅したとされることもある<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=特集〜戦後70年 和歌山大空襲 ー7.9の記憶ー |url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/ |website=ニュース和歌山 |access-date=2022-07-07 |language=ja}}</ref>）。
市民は4,000[[坪]]の空き地になっていた[[和歌山県庁舎|旧和歌山県庁舎跡]]（現：汀公園）に避難していたが、そこを[[火災]]による熱風が襲ったため、そこだけで748人もの死者を出してしまった<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|和書|title=総務省｜一般戦災死没者の追悼｜和歌山市における戦災の状況（和歌山県） |url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html |website=総務省 |access-date=2022-07-07 |language=ja}}</ref>。また城北橋や中橋の下にあたる、[[市堀川]]の水中に逃げ込んだ者もいたが、満潮時であり多数の死者を出した。
翌[[7月10日]]1時48分に爆撃は終了し、2時30分頃にはB-29は[[潮岬]]上空から南方洋上へ去っていった。3時25分に[[空襲警報]]は解除された。
戦後、汀公園と城北橋付近には供養塔が、中橋付近には地蔵尊が建立されている。
=== 被害 === * 死者 - 1,208人 * 重傷者 - 1,560人 * 軽傷者 - 3,000人 * 行方不明者 - 216人 * 被災者 - 113,548人 * 焼失家屋 - 31,137戸 : （[[7月16日]]の[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]警報局宛報告による） アメリカ軍の記録には「和歌山市の建物密集地4[[平方マイル]]のうち52.5%を破壊。焼夷弾800.3[[トン]]を使用した」とあり、B-29搭乗員の記録にも「何回も大きな[[爆発]]と共に、いくつもの白い[[光|閃光]]が発生し、爆撃目標地点上空では[[乱気流]]が発生した。20,000フィートにも達する煙の柱をともなう猛烈な火災が全市で発生した」とあり、空襲の凄まじさが窺える。なお、死者数は1101人とする説<ref name=":1" />、1400人とする説もある<ref name=":0" />。
=== 被災文化財 === ;和歌山城焼失 7月9日の和歌山市の市街地に対する大規模空襲により、和歌山の象徴であった[[和歌山城]]が炎上の末に焼失した。城内には旧国宝の[[天守]]をはじめとする多数の国宝や建築物などがあったが、岡口門など数棟を残して全焼した。戦後の[[1958年]]に和歌山市民の熱意により天守は鉄筋コンクリート構造で外観復元された。 ;その他の被災建造物 *[[松生院]]（旧国宝） *[[報恩寺 (和歌山市)|報恩寺]]
=== 著名な被災者 === * [[ジャニー喜多川]] - [[ジャニーズ事務所]]創業者・社長<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=ジャニーさん反戦訴え 日本と米国で2度の戦争体験|newspaper=ニッカンスポーツ・コム|agency=日刊スポーツ新聞社|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[メリー喜多川]] - [[ジャニーズ事務所]]名誉会長。 * [[黒沢良]] - [[声優]]、[[ナレーター]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == {{No footnotes|date=2018年6月}} * 『和歌山市史』（和歌山市史編纂室）
== 関連項目 == * [[日本本土空襲]] - [[和歌山県]]内では、この他にも[[新宮空襲|新宮]]、[[海南空襲|海南]]、[[田辺空襲|田辺]]などが空襲被害に遭っている。 ** [[岐阜空襲]] - 和歌山大空襲とほぼ同時に行われた空襲。 ** [[仙台空襲]] - 同上。 ** [[大阪大空襲#堺大空襲（第6回大阪大空襲） - 7月10日|堺大空襲]] - 7月10日未明より、和歌山大空襲に連続して行われた空襲。 *[[太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔]]
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:わかやまたいくうしゆう}} [[Category:日本本土空襲]] [[Category:和歌山市の歴史|たいくうしゆう]] [[Category:和歌山県の歴史|たいくうしゆう]] [[Category:1945年7月]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和歌山大空襲]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11417327]] Wikidata name: Bombing of Wakayama [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|air raids on Wakayama]] conducted by the [[:en:United States Army Air Forces|United States Army Air Forces]] during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale [[:en:Strategic bombing|strategic bombing]] that targeted the city center of [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]] from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[:en:July 10|July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as [[:en:Iwade, Wakayama|Iwade Town]] (now Iwade City) and [[:en:Kokawa, Wakayama|Kokawa Town]] (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Wakayama Station|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山駅|1=Wakayama Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1044107}}<ref group="Note">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[:en:August 14|August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple [[:en:Boeing B-29 Superfortress|B-29 Superfortress]] bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from [[:en:Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|West Field]]. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|qq=|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of [[:en:Kinokawa Station|Kinokawa Station]], [[:en:Wakayamashi Station|Wakayama City Station]], the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|qq=|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|qq=|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|qq=|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|qq=|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[:en:July 16|July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]], a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[:en:1958|1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|qq=|1=Shōjō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|qq=|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[:en:Johnny Kitagawa|Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[:en:Mary Kitagawa|Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

[[ja:和歌山大空襲]]
[[Category:July 1945]] [[Category:History of Wakayama]] [[Category:World War II strategic bombing of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11417327}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和歌山大空襲]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11417327]] Wikidata name: Bombing of Wakayama [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|air raids on Wakayama]] conducted by the [[:en:United States Army Air Forces|United States Army Air Forces]] during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale [[:en:Strategic bombing|strategic bombing]] that targeted the city center of [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]] from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[:en:July 10|July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as [[:en:Iwade, Wakayama|Iwade Town]] (now Iwade City) and [[:en:Kokawa, Wakayama|Kokawa Town]] (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山駅|1=Wakayama Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1044107}}<ref group="Note">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[:en:August 14|August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple [[:en:Boeing B-29 Superfortress|B-29 Superfortress]] bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from [[:en:Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|West Field]]. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of [[:en:Kinokawa Station|Kinokawa Station]], [[:en:Wakayamashi Station|Wakayama City Station]], the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[:en:July 16|July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]], a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[:en:1958|1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[:en:Johnny Kitagawa|Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[:en:Mary Kitagawa|Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of [[:en:Kinokawa Station|Kinokawa Station]], [[:en:Wakayamashi Station|Wakayama City Station]], the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[:en:July 16|July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]], a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[:en:1958|1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[:en:Johnny Kitagawa|Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[:en:Mary Kitagawa|Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office) == See Also == * [[:en:Air raids on Japan|Air raids on Japan]] - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kainan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were also bombed. ''' [[:en:Bombing of Gifu in World War II|Gifu Air Raid]] - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' [[:en:Bombing of Sendai in World War II|Sendai Air Raid]] - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2814395}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/War Victims Memorial Tower|1=Cenotaph for the city air-raid victims of the Pacific War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443976}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和歌山大空襲]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11417327]] Wikidata name: Bombing of Wakayama [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|air raids on Wakayama]] conducted by the [[:en:United States Army Air Forces|United States Army Air Forces]] during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale [[:en:Strategic bombing|strategic bombing]] that targeted the city center of [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]] from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[:en:July 10|July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as [[:en:Iwade, Wakayama|Iwade Town]] (now Iwade City) and [[:en:Kokawa, Wakayama|Kokawa Town]] (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山駅|1=Wakayama Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1044107}}<ref group="Note">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[:en:August 14|August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple [[:en:Boeing B-29 Superfortress|B-29 Superfortress]] bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from [[:en:Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|West Field]]. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of [[:en:Kinokawa Station|Kinokawa Station]], [[:en:Wakayamashi Station|Wakayama City Station]], the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[:en:July 16|July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]], a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[:en:1958|1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[:en:Johnny Kitagawa|Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[:en:Mary Kitagawa|Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2814395}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/War Victims Memorial Tower|1=Cenotaph for the city air-raid victims of the Pacific War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443976}}

[[Category:July 1945]] [[Category:History of Wakayama]] [[Category:World War II strategic bombing of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11417327}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和歌山大空襲]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11417327]] Wikidata name: Bombing of Wakayama [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|air raids on Wakayama]] conducted by the [[:en:United States Army Air Forces|United States Army Air Forces]] during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]]. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale [[:en:Strategic bombing|strategic bombing]] that targeted the city center of [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] and [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]] from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of [[:en:July 10|July 10]].
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as [[:en:Iwade, Wakayama|Iwade Town]] (now Iwade City) and [[:en:Kokawa, Wakayama|Kokawa Town]] (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山駅|1=Wakayama Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1044107}}<ref group="Note">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until [[:en:August 14|August 14]], approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple [[:en:Boeing B-29 Superfortress|B-29 Superfortress]] bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from [[:en:Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|West Field]]. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of [[:en:Kinokawa Station|Kinokawa Station]], [[:en:Wakayamashi Station|Wakayama City Station]], the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on [[:en:July 16|July 16]]) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]], a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[:en:1958|1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[:en:Johnny Kitagawa|Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[:en:Mary Kitagawa|Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office) == See Also == * [[:en:Air raids on Japan|Air raids on Japan]] - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kainan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were also bombed. ''' [[:en:Bombing of Gifu in World War II|Gifu Air Raid]] - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' [[:en:Bombing of Sendai in World War II|Sendai Air Raid]] - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2814395}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/War Victims Memorial Tower|1=Cenotaph for the city air-raid victims of the Pacific War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443976}}

The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in [[:en:1958|1958]] through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * [[:en:Johnny Kitagawa|Johnny Kitagawa]] - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * [[:en:Mary Kitagawa|Mary Kitagawa]] - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

''' [[:en:Bombing of Gifu in World War II|Gifu Air Raid]] - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' [[:en:Bombing of Sendai in World War II|Sendai Air Raid]] - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2814395}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/War Victims Memorial Tower|1=Cenotaph for the city air-raid victims of the Pacific War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443976}}
