revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和歌山大空襲]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和歌山大空襲]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:和歌山大空襲]]
{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=air raids on Wakayama}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|en|United States Army Air Forces|WD=Q741723|lt=United States Army Air Forces}} during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|Strategic bombing|en|Strategic bombing|WD=Q841418|lt=strategic bombing}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama City}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of {{ill|July 10|en|July 10|WD=Q2689|lt=July 10}}.
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade Town}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa, Wakayama|en|Kokawa, Wakayama|WD=Q6426573|lt=Kokawa Town}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山駅|1=Wakayama Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1044107}}<ref group="Note">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until {{ill|August 14|en|August 14|WD=Q2775|lt=August 14}}, approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|en|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|WD=Q184870|lt=B-29 Superfortress}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|en|Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|WD=Q325652|lt=West Field}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|en|Kinokawa Station|WD=Q11604938|lt=Kinokawa Station}}, {{ill|Wakayamashi Station|en|Wakayamashi Station|WD=Q3455721|lt=Wakayama City Station}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on {{ill|July 16|en|July 16|WD=Q2731|lt=July 16}}) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|en|Wakayama Castle|WD=Q1151269|lt=Wakayama Castle}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958}} through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * {{ill|Johnny Kitagawa|en|Johnny Kitagawa|WD=Q1321093|lt=Johnny Kitagawa}} - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * {{ill|Mary Kitagawa|en|Mary Kitagawa|WD=Q82437650|lt=Mary Kitagawa}} - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office) == See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|en|Air raids on Japan|WD=Q590545|lt=Air raids on Japan}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kainan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Bombing of Gifu in World War II|en|Bombing of Gifu in World War II|WD=Q11471867|lt=Gifu Air Raid}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Bombing of Sendai in World War II|en|Bombing of Sendai in World War II|WD=Q11378048|lt=Sendai Air Raid}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2814395}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/War Victims Memorial Tower|1=Cenotaph for the city air-raid victims of the Pacific War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443976}}
{{japanese-history-stub}}

{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=air raids on Wakayama|1=Wakayama (city)}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|en|United States Army Air Forces|WD=Q741723|lt=United States Army Air Forces|1=United States Army Air Forces}} during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|Strategic bombing|en|Strategic bombing|WD=Q841418|lt=strategic bombing|1=Strategic bombing}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama City|1=Wakayama (city)}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of {{ill|July 10|en|July 10|WD=Q2689|lt=July 10|1=July 10}}.
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade Town|1=Iwade, Wakayama}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa, Wakayama|en|Kokawa, Wakayama|WD=Q6426573|lt=Kokawa Town|1=Kokawa, Wakayama}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山駅|1=Wakayama Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1044107}}<ref group="Note">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until {{ill|August 14|en|August 14|WD=Q2775|lt=August 14|1=August 14}}, approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|en|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|WD=Q184870|lt=B-29 Superfortress|1=Boeing B-29 Superfortress}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|en|Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|WD=Q325652|lt=West Field|1=Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|en|Kinokawa Station|WD=Q11604938|lt=Kinokawa Station|1=Kinokawa Station}}, {{ill|Wakayamashi Station|en|Wakayamashi Station|WD=Q3455721|lt=Wakayama City Station|1=Wakayamashi Station}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on {{ill|July 16|en|July 16|WD=Q2731|lt=July 16|1=July 16}}) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|en|Wakayama Castle|WD=Q1151269|lt=Wakayama Castle|1=Wakayama Castle}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}} through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * {{ill|Johnny Kitagawa|en|Johnny Kitagawa|WD=Q1321093|lt=Johnny Kitagawa|1=Johnny Kitagawa}} - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * {{ill|Mary Kitagawa|en|Mary Kitagawa|WD=Q82437650|lt=Mary Kitagawa|1=Mary Kitagawa}} - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|en|Kinokawa Station|WD=Q11604938|lt=Kinokawa Station|1=Kinokawa Station}}, {{ill|Wakayamashi Station|en|Wakayamashi Station|WD=Q3455721|lt=Wakayama City Station|1=Wakayamashi Station}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on {{ill|July 16|en|July 16|WD=Q2731|lt=July 16|1=July 16}}) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|en|Wakayama Castle|WD=Q1151269|lt=Wakayama Castle|1=Wakayama Castle}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}} through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * {{ill|Johnny Kitagawa|en|Johnny Kitagawa|WD=Q1321093|lt=Johnny Kitagawa|1=Johnny Kitagawa}} - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * {{ill|Mary Kitagawa|en|Mary Kitagawa|WD=Q82437650|lt=Mary Kitagawa|1=Mary Kitagawa}} - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.
== Notes ==

== Further Reading == {{No footnotes|date=June 2018}} * *History of Wakayama City* (Wakayama City History Compilation Office) == See Also == * {{ill|Air raids on Japan|en|Air raids on Japan|WD=Q590545|lt=Air raids on Japan|1=Air raids on Japan}} - Other locations in Wakayama Prefecture, such as {{ill|Shingū|ja|新宮空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kainan|ja|海南空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kainan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Tanabe|ja|田辺空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were also bombed. ''' {{ill|Bombing of Gifu in World War II|en|Bombing of Gifu in World War II|WD=Q11471867|lt=Gifu Air Raid|1=Bombing of Gifu in World War II}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Bombing of Sendai in World War II|en|Bombing of Sendai in World War II|WD=Q11378048|lt=Sendai Air Raid|1=Bombing of Sendai in World War II}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2814395}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/War Victims Memorial Tower|1=Cenotaph for the city air-raid victims of the Pacific War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443976}}

revision.text:
{{Pathnav|World War II|Pacific War|Battle of Mainland Japan|Air raids on Japan|frame=1}} '''Wakayama Air Raid''' (和歌山大空襲, Wakayama Daikūshū) refers to a series of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=air raids on Wakayama|1=Wakayama (city)}} conducted by the {{ill|United States Army Air Forces|en|United States Army Air Forces|WD=Q741723|lt=United States Army Air Forces|1=United States Army Air Forces}} during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}. The term often specifically refers to the large-scale {{ill|Strategic bombing|en|Strategic bombing|WD=Q841418|lt=strategic bombing|1=Strategic bombing}} that targeted the city center of {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama City|1=Wakayama (city)}} from the late night of [[July 9, 1945]], to the early hours of {{ill|July 10|en|July 10|WD=Q2689|lt=July 10|1=July 10}}.
== Chronology of the Raids == === Air Raids Before July 9, 1945 === The first air raid on Wakayama Prefecture occurred in April 1942, targeting areas such as {{ill|Iwade, Wakayama|en|Iwade, Wakayama|WD=Q579623|lt=Iwade Town|1=Iwade, Wakayama}} (now Iwade City) and {{ill|Kokawa, Wakayama|en|Kokawa, Wakayama|WD=Q6426573|lt=Kokawa Town|1=Kokawa, Wakayama}} (now Kinokawa City).
On [[January 9, 1945]], the area around {{ill|Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja|東和歌山駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Wakayama Station|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山駅|1=Wakayama Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1044107}}<ref group="Note">Renamed Wakayama Station in [[1968]].</ref> was bombed. Over the following months, until {{ill|August 14|en|August 14|WD=Q2775|lt=August 14|1=August 14}}, approximately a dozen air raids were conducted.
Various coastal cities and inland areas in Wakayama Prefecture, including port facilities and oil infrastructure, also suffered from strategic bombings and strafing.
=== The Air Raid of July 9 === On the evening of July 9, 1945, around 5:00 PM, multiple {{ill|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|en|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|WD=Q184870|lt=B-29 Superfortress|1=Boeing B-29 Superfortress}} bombers and a single weather observation aircraft took off from {{ill|Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|en|Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands|WD=Q325652|lt=West Field|1=Tinian, Northern Mariana Islands}}. At 9:10 PM, a warning was issued for Wakayama City and the surrounding seas, with reports of American planes a11roaching Kumano offshore. However, as the planes did not immediately arrive, many citizens let down their guard. At 10:25 PM, an air raid warning was issued for the entirety of Wakayama Prefecture, and at 10:30 PM, a warning was issued for the surrounding seas of Kishu.
By 11:00 PM, American aircraft began flying at altitudes between 10,200 and 11,600 feet (about 3,100–3,500 meters), dro11ing {{ill|illumination flares|ja|照明弾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/illumination flares|1=Star shell|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985168}} over the area near the mouth of the Minato River in Wakayama's western region, signaling the start of the bombing. Subsequent targets included the vicinity of {{ill|Kinokawa Station|en|Kinokawa Station|WD=Q11604938|lt=Kinokawa Station|1=Kinokawa Station}}, {{ill|Wakayamashi Station|en|Wakayamashi Station|WD=Q3455721|lt=Wakayama City Station|1=Wakayamashi Station}}, the {{ill|Burakuri Arcade|ja|ぶらくり丁商店街|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Burakuri Arcade|1=Burakurichō Shopping Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213932}}, and the areas around the {{ill|Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|ja|和歌山県庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Prefectural Government Office|1=Wakayama Prefectural Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417410}} and {{ill|Wakayama City Hall|ja|和歌山市役所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama City Hall|1=Wakayama City Hall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417332}}. These sites were devastated by incendiary and oil-gel bombs, with the city center suffering almost complete destruction (some reports estimate 70% of the area was destroyed<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Special Feature: Remembering the Wakayama Air Raid - The Memory of July 9|url=https://www.nwn.jp/news/201507040203daikuu/|website=News Wakayama|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref>).
Citizens sought refuge in a 4,000-tsubo (approximately 13,223 square meters) open space at the former Wakayama Prefectural Office site (now {{ill|Migiwa Park|ja|汀公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Migiwa Park|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but a firestorm claimed the lives of 748 people there alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications - General War Victim Memorial Information: Wakayama City (Wakayama Prefecture)|url=https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/daijinkanbou/sensai/situation/state/kinki_14.html|website=Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications|access-date=2022-07-07|language=ja}}</ref> Others sought shelter under the {{ill|Shirokita Bridge|ja|城北橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirokita Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nakabashi Bridge in {{ill|Ichihorigawa River|ja|市堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihorigawa River|1=Ichihori River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923161}}, but high tide caused further casualties.
The bombing ended at 1:48 AM on July 10, and by around 2:30 AM, the B-29s left the area via the skies over Cape Shionomisaki, heading south. The air raid warning was lifted at 3:25 AM.
== Damage == * Deaths: 1,208 * Severe injuries: 1,560 * Minor injuries: 3,000 * Missing: 216 * Displaced persons: 113,548 * Houses destroyed: 31,137 : (According to a report to the Ministry of Home Affairs on {{ill|July 16|en|July 16|WD=Q2731|lt=July 16|1=July 16}}) American records indicate that "52.5% of Wakayama City's densely populated 4 square miles was destroyed, with 800.3 tons of incendiary bombs used." Pilots also reported "multiple large explosions and flashes of light, turbulent air currents over target zones, and smoke columns reaching 20,000 feet, accompanied by fierce fires throughout the city." Casualty estimates vary, with some sources suggesting 1,101 deaths<ref name=":1" />, while others estimate up to 1,400.<ref name=":0" />
=== Cultural Property Damage === ; Destruction of Wakayama Castle The {{ill|Wakayama Castle|en|Wakayama Castle|WD=Q1151269|lt=Wakayama Castle|1=Wakayama Castle}}, a symbol of Wakayama, was set ablaze during the July 9 air raid and destroyed. Numerous national treasures and historic buildings, including the {{ill|main keep|ja|Tenshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/main keep|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the castle, were lost, though a few structures, such as the Okaguchi Gate, survived. The keep was later reconstructed in reinforced concrete in {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}} through the dedication of Wakayama citizens. ; Other Damaged Structures * {{ill|Shōseiin|ja|松生院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōseiin|1=Shōjō-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531943}} (formerly a national treasure) * {{ill|Hōon-ji Temple|ja|報恩寺_(和歌山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōon-ji Temple|1=Hōon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11428040}}
=== Notable Victims === * {{ill|Johnny Kitagawa|en|Johnny Kitagawa|WD=Q1321093|lt=Johnny Kitagawa|1=Johnny Kitagawa}} - Founder and President of Johnny & Associates.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/entertainment/news/201907090001413.html|title=Johnny Kitagawa's Anti-War Message: Experiences of Two Wars in Japan and the U.S.|newspaper=Nikkan Sports|date=2019-07-09|accessdate=2019-07-10}}</ref> * {{ill|Mary Kitagawa|en|Mary Kitagawa|WD=Q82437650|lt=Mary Kitagawa|1=Mary Kitagawa}} - Honorary President of Johnny & Associates. * [[Ryō Kurosawa]] - Voice actor and narrator.

''' {{ill|Bombing of Gifu in World War II|en|Bombing of Gifu in World War II|WD=Q11471867|lt=Gifu Air Raid|1=Bombing of Gifu in World War II}} - Conducted almost simultaneously with the Wakayama Air Raid. ''' {{ill|Bombing of Sendai in World War II|en|Bombing of Sendai in World War II|WD=Q11378048|lt=Sendai Air Raid|1=Bombing of Sendai in World War II}} - Also occurred around the same time. ''' {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2814395}} - Followed the Wakayama Air Raid in the early hours of July 10. * {{ill|War Victims Memorial Tower|ja|太平洋戦全国戦災都市空爆死没者慰霊塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/War Victims Memorial Tower|1=Cenotaph for the city air-raid victims of the Pacific War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443976}}
